texts: I feel a little nervous about my presentation
responses: Pre-presentation nerves are normal! Practice your opening lines, take three deep breaths before starting, and remember - the audience wants you to succeed.

texts: I'm worried about my exam results
responses: Waiting for results is stressful. What's done is done. Focus on what you can control right now. Distract yourself with a hobby or exercise.

texts: My heart is racing and I can't calm down
responses: Let's do the 4-7-8 breathing technique. Breathe in for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8. Repeat 4 times. Feel that? Your body is calming down.

texts: I keep having panic attacks
responses: Panic attacks are terrifying but they always pass. Ground yourself: Name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste. You're safe.

texts: I can't stop worrying about everything
responses: Generalized anxiety is exhausting. Let's do a 'worry dump' - write down everything you're worried about. Then identify what you can control vs what you can't.

texts: I can't leave my house because of anxiety
responses: That sounds incredibly difficult. Start small - just open your front door. Tomorrow, step outside for 1 minute. Gradual exposure works. Consider seeing a therapist who specializes in anxiety.

texts: I've been feeling down lately
responses: Everyone feels down sometimes. Have you been sleeping and eating well? Sometimes small self-care actions make a big difference.

texts: I've lost interest in my hobbies
responses: Loss of interest is common. Try the '5-minute rule' - do something you used to enjoy for just 5 minutes. No pressure to enjoy it, just try.

texts: I feel empty and hopeless
responses: I'm sorry you're feeling this way. You're not alone. Can you do one small thing for yourself today? Drink water, eat something, step outside for 2 minutes?

texts: Nothing makes me happy anymore
responses: Depression steals joy. What used to bring you happiness? Even if it doesn't now, can you try it for 5 minutes? Sometimes the feeling follows the action.

texts: I don't want to get out of bed
responses: Getting out of bed is a win. Can we start with just sitting up? Or moving to the couch? One small step is still progress.

texts: I don't see any point in living
responses: I'm very concerned about you. Your life matters. Please reach out to the Suicide & Crisis Lifeline at 988. They have trained counselors who can help right now.

texts: I feel completely worthless
responses: Depression lies to us. You have worth. Can you name one person who would be affected if you weren't here? Please call 988 or a trusted person right now.

texts: I'm so stressed with work deadlines
responses: Work stress is real. Let's prioritize: What's urgent? What can wait? What can you delegate? Take a 5-minute break right now.

texts: I'm overwhelmed with everything
responses: When everything feels too much, let's break it down. What's ONE thing you can do in the next 5 minutes? Focus only on that.

texts: I feel burned out from school
responses: Academic burnout is real. When did you last take a real break? Not studying, not scrolling - actual rest. Your brain needs recovery time.

texts: I'm stressed about my relationship
responses: Relationship stress hurts. Can you communicate openly with your partner? Sometimes saying 'I'm struggling' is a good first step.

texts: I feel so energetic and unstoppable
responses: High energy can feel great, but be careful. Are you sleeping? Not sleeping can trigger mania. Try to maintain a consistent sleep schedule.

texts: My moods keep swinging wildly
responses: Mood swings are exhausting. Have you been tracking your moods? A mood diary can help identify patterns. This is important to share with your doctor.

texts: I'm in a deep depression after feeling so high
responses: The crash after mania is brutal. Be gentle with yourself. This is part of the bipolar cycle. Reach out to your psychiatrist if you haven't already.

texts: I'm doing okay today
responses: I'm glad to hear that! What's one positive thing that happened today? Let's appreciate those moments.

texts: Just checking in
responses: Thanks for checking in! I'm here. How are you feeling right now? What's on your mind?

texts: I had a good day
responses: That's wonderful! What made it good? Let's celebrate those moments and remember them for harder days.

texts: I'm feeling much better today
responses: I'm so glad! What helped you feel better? Keep doing that. You're making progress.

texts: I keep having flashbacks of something bad
responses: Flashbacks are terrifying. Ground yourself: What year is it? Where are you? Look around and name what you see. You're safe now. EMDR therapy can help with trauma.

texts: I can't stop thinking about what happened
responses: Intrusive thoughts are exhausting. Try writing them down to get them out of your head. Trauma-focused therapy can help process these memories.

texts: I lost someone I love and I can't cope
responses: Grief is heavy. There's no timeline. Let yourself feel whatever comes up. Can you share a favorite memory of them? Sometimes talking helps.

texts: I'm still grieving after months
responses: Grief doesn't have a timeline. Some days will be harder. Grief counseling can help. You don't have to go through this alone.
